Facts.text: Vigilius ends his reign as Catholic Pope
First Crusaders arrive in Jerusalem
Earliest report of ball lightning in London by Benedictine monk Gervase of Christ Church Cathedral Priory, Canterbury
Rotterdam is founded in the Netherlands
King Ladislaw of Naples occupies Rome
Troops of the Republic of Venice capture Udine, ending the independence of the Patriarchate of Aquileia
Peace of Andres Treaty ends the stalemated Italian War of 1542–1546 of France and the Ottoman Empire against the Holy Roman Empire and England, as well as England's dispute with Scotland and Ireland
England declares war on France
Munsee Esopus make a surprise attack on Dutch settlement of Wiltwijck (Kingston, Hudson Valley, NY), killing 25 colonists and taking 45 hostage
Boston's First Baptist Church, one of the oldest Baptist churches in America, is founded [NS March 1]
Battle of Solebay: Dutch Admiral Michiel de Ruyter defeats a French-English fleet
English invasion army under Thomas Tollemache reaches Brest
Gordon Riots: anti-Catholic riots across London, hundreds die and fires burn across the city
French peasants stone the Army in Grenbole, an event known as the Day of the Tiles
Jews of Pesaro Italy fast commemorating murder of Jews
Asian cholera reaches Quebec, brought by Irish immigrants, and kills about 6,000 people in Lower Canada
Hawaiian Declaration of Rights is signed
Workmen start laying track for Market Street Railroad, San Francisco
Skirmish at Union Church, Virginia (Peninsular)
The United States and Britain agree to suppress the slave trade
Mexico City captured by French troops
1,800 Fenian raiders are repelled back to the United States after they loot and plunder around Saint-Armand and Frelighsburg, Quebec
California Rifle and Pistol Association founded
War of the Pacific: The Battle of Arica, assault and capture of Morro de Arica (Arica Cape), ends the Campaña del Desierto (Desert Campaign)
Monotype type-casting machine patented by Tolbert Lanston, Washington, D.C.
Creole shoemaker Homer Plessy buys whites-only train ticket in New Orleans in act of civil disobedience - results in landmark U.S. Supreme Court case Plessy v. Ferguson (1896)
John J. Doyle of Clev Spiders is 1st to pinch hit in a baseball game
Republican convention in Minneapolis begins
Social Democracy of America party holds 1st national convention, Chicago
Boer General Christian de Law occupies British rail depot at Roodewal

Chicago Cubs score 11 runs in the 1st inning of a 19-0 drubbing of the New York Giants off future Baseball Hall of Famers Christy Mathewson and Joe McGinnity; worst beating in Giants franchise history
Famous Cunard passenger liner Lusitania launches
Cleveland Industrial Exposition opens
St Pius X encyclical "On Indians of South America"
US army tests 1st machine gun mounted on a plane
1st verifiable ascent of main summit of Denali (Mt McKinley), North America's highest mountain led by Hudson Stuck and Harry Karstens
Melvin Jones and a number of other Chicago businessmen found Lions Clubs International, now the largest service organization in the world
The British detonate mines beneath the German-held Messines Ridge, in the Ypres area
Sette giugno: Riot in Malta; four are killed.
Swedish government of Ekman forms
Margaret Bondfield becomes 1st British female cabinet minister (Labour)
Vatican City becomes a sovereign state
At 47, Brooklyn pitcher Jack Quinn becomes oldest player in MLB history to record an extra-base hit (double) as the Dodgers beat Chicago Cubs, 9-2
Yanks beat Indians 5-4 in 16; longest game without a strikeout
1st play telecast with original Broadway cast, "Susan & God"
Boeing 314 Clipper flying boat 1st flown (Eddie Allen)
British/French troops evacuate Narvik
Germany Army marches into Sebastopol, Russia
Japanese troops land on Attu, Aleutian Islands
USS Yorktown sinks near Midway Island
Achilles Veen soccer team forms in Veen
British forces attack Bréville in France during the Normandy invasion while other British forces attempt to encircle Caen
Canadian 50th Division occupies Bayeux during the Normandy invasion

MLB Pittsburgh Pirates players attempt to unionize and vote 20-16 to go on strike rather than play the New York Giants; vote fell short of required 2/3 majority and union fizzles, and Pirates win 10-5 at Forbes Field, Pittsburghn
Communist complete takeover of Czechoslovakia; President Bernes resigns
KVP wins Dutch Second-Parliamentary election
1st color network telecast in compatible color (Boston Massachusetts)
WDAU (now WYOU) TV channel 22 in Scranton Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania (CBS) begins
First microbiology laboratory dedicated in New Brunswick, New Jersey
"$64,000 Question" hosted by Hal March, premieres on CBS TV
Singapore government of Marshall resigns
Mickey Stewart holds 7 cricket catches for Surrey v Northants
Battles between Turkish & Greeks Cypriots break out
KLX-AM in Oakland California changes call letters to KEWB (now KNEW)
1st Rolling Stones TV appearance (Thank Your Lucky Stars) & release 1st single, "Come On"
The Beatles travel canals of Amsterdam, with a substitute for Ringo
Gemini 4 completes 62 orbits
Morocco King Hassan suspends constitution, grabs power
Sony Corp introduces its home video tape recorder, priced at $995
The Supreme Court of the United States decides on Griswold v. Connecticut, effectively legalizing the use of contraception by married couples
Israel captures Wailing Wall in East Jerusalem, Jericho and Bethlehem
NY Yankees draft Ron Blomberg #1
Two members of American psychedelic rock band "Moby Grape" arrested for contributing to delinquency of minors, charges were later dropped
The body of assassinated US SenatorRobert F. Kennedylies in state at St. Patrick's Cathedral, New York
Tommy James and the Shondells release single "Crystal Blue Persuasion"
Washington Senators draft Jeff Burroughs #1
22nd Emmy Awards: Marcus Welby, Robert Young & Susan Hampshire win
British rock band The Who's perform their rock opera "Tommy" at the Metropolitan Opera House at NYC's Lincoln Center
Soviet Soyuz 11 crew completes 1st transfer to orbiting Salyut space station
Hsan Hua, Zen teacher, conducted 1st ordination ceremony in America
Jim Jacobs and Warren Casey's musical "Grease," starring Barry Bostwick, Carole Demas, and Adrienne Barbeau, opens at the Broadhurst Theatre in NYC; runs for 3,388 performances
Pirate's Gene Alley bases-loaded walk, beats Padres 1-0 in 18th
Rangers draft Texas high school pitcher David Clyde #1
MLB San Diego Padres draft Brown University shortstop Bill Almon #1
Sony introduces the Betamax videocassette recorder for sale to the public
American singer and orange juice spokeswoman Anita Bryant leads successful crusade against gay rights law in Miami, Florida
White Sox draft Harold Baines #1
52nd National Spelling Bee: Katie Kerwin wins spelling maculature
Bhaskara 1, Indian Earth resources/meteorology satellite, launched
Portugal government of Mota Pinto resigns
"Cars" by Gary Numan hits #9
Jeff Norman runs world record 50k (2:48:06)
35th Tony Awards: "Amadeus" (play) & "42nd Street" (musical) win
Israeli F-15/F-16 jets destroy an alleged Iraqi plutonium production facility
Steve Garveyplays in his 1,000th consecutive game & goes 0-for-4
Pirates draft 3rd baseman Jeff King #1
41st Tony Awards: "Fences" (play) and "Les Misérables" (musical) win
Andy Van Slyke HRs off R McDowell in both ends of double header
Aluminum contaminates Cornwall's water supply
23-year-old olympic barefoot South African runner Zola Budd retires
Atlanta Fulton County Comm approves $210M stadium for the Falcons
First baseball game to start outdoors and end indoors as Toronto Blue Jays' stadium closes roof during game at 8:48 and beat Brewers 4-2
For one second this morning, the time is 01:23:45, 6-7-89
Suriname SLM-173 (DC-8) crashes near Paramaribo Airport killing 173
Mount Pinatubo (Philippines) erupts for 1st time
NASA's Extreme Ultraviolet Explorer (EUVE) launches
American singer Prince celebrates his birthday by changing his name to an unpronounceable symbol
Rock and Roll Hall of Fame breaks ground for their museum in Cleveland, Ohio
The long range Boeing 777 enters service with United Airlines
Julia and Noah wed on "All My Children"
Russian reconnaissance satellite Cosmos 2344 launches from Baikonur Cosmodrome in Kazakhstan using a four-stage Proton-K rocket
Stanley Cup Final, Joe Louis Arena, Detroit, MI: Detroit Red Wings edge Philadelphia Flyers, 2-1 for a 4-0 series sweep; 8th title in Wings' franchise history and first since 1955
52nd Tony Awards: "The Lion King" (musical) and '"Art" (play) win
James Byrd, Jr. is dragged to death by Shawn Allen Berry, Lawrence Russel Brewer, and John William King in Jasper, Texas in a racially-motivated hate crime
BP announces that it will build a new $600-million platform offshore Trinidad that is expected to double the company's production of natural gas there by 2004
Stanley Cup Final, Amalie Arena, Tampa, FL: Tampa Bay Lightning beat Calgary Flames, 2-1 for 4-3 series win; Tampa Bay's 1st-ever appearance in final
British Houses of Parliament temporarily shut down due to anthrax alert
Vatnajökull National Park in Iceland formed as second-largest European national park, combining Skaftafell and Jökulsárgljúfur parks and the Vatnajökull ice cap (World Heritage Site 2019)
63rd Tony Awards: "Billy Elliot the Musical" (musical) and "God of Carnage" (play) win
A bus falls into a ravine in La Paz, Bolivia, killing 16 and injuring 32 people
Credit rating agency Fitch downgrades Spain's credit rating to BBB in the wake of international bailout speculation
18 people are killed and 14 are injured after a bus plunges off a mountain road in Himachel Pradesh, India
42 people are killed and 30 are injured after a bus catches fire in Xiamen, China
-8 41st G7 summit held in Schloss Elmau, Bavaria
69th Tony Awards: "The Curious Incident of the Dog in the Night-Time" (play) and "Fun Home" (musical) each win 5 awards
"Harry Potter and the Cursed Child", a play written by Jack Thorne with JK Rowling, premieres in London
Car bomb attack on a police bus in central Istanbul kills 11
Earliest-ever evidence of Homo Sapiens from Jebel Irhoud, Morocco unearthed by archaeologists published in "Nature", at 300,000 years old
Police warn bald men against attacks in Mozambique after 5 men murdered for the gold believed in their heads
Baltimore ex-police sergeant Wayne Earl Jenkins, head of a rogue police unit sentenced to 25 years for robbery and racketeering
Mars Curiosity Rover finds organic matter, including methane, on Mars in studies published in journal "Science"
Stanley Cup Final, T-Mobile Arena, Las Vegas, NV: Washington Capitals beat Las Vegas Golden Knights, 4-3 for a 4-1 series victory; first title in franchise history
More than four million Venezuelans have left their country since 2015 due to its economic crisis according to the UN
Black Lives Matter Protests continue worldwide in large numbers, In Bristol England statue of 17th century slave trader Edward Colston pulled down
COVID-19 global death toll passes 400,000 with confirmed cases at 6,973,195 according to Johns Hopkins figures
US National Guard pulled out of Washington D.C. and curfew ends in New York as anti-racism protests stay largely peaceful
Auckland named world's most livable city (due to success with COVID) and Damascus named the worst by The Economist Intelligence Unit
Multicellular organisms (bdelloid rotifer) frozen for 24,000 years in Siberia to return to life after Russian scientists have them thawed
Two passenger trains collide in Pakistan, killing at least 40 people in Sindh province
UN International Labor Director says global impact of the pandemic four times worse than 2008 Economic Crisis, pushed 100 million workers into poverty
New study reveals Shark Bay sea grass, off Western Australia, may be world's largest plant, covering 77 square miles of sea floor, having cloned itself for 4,500 years
“For many countries, recession will be hard to avoid.” says World Bank President David Malpass, with prediction world growth in 2022 will slow to 2.9% (down from 5.7%)
Britain's Boxford Timber identified as UK's oldest decoratively carved wood at 6,000 years old by radio carbon dating after being found in West Berkshire during house renovations
157th Belmont Stakes: Junior Alvarado aboard Sovereignty wins in 2:00.69 at slightly shorter course in Saratoga Springs, New York, due to renovation construction at usual Belmont Park location
Field of the Cloth of Gold; meeting begins between EnglishHenry VIIIand KingFrancis I of Franceat Balinghem, France
PopeClement VIIsurrenders to EmperorCharles V's armies
Second parliament ofKing James Idissolves after passing no legislation, earning itself the name of the Addled Parliament
English KingCharles Iratifies the Petition of Right
14-year-old Mughal princeAurangzeb, the future Emperor Alamgir I, survives an elephant attack
Louis XIVis crowned King of France
An earthquake in Port Royal, Jamaica, kills 3,000 and sinks the cemetery whereHenry Morganwas buried into the sea
American frontiersmanDaniel Booneascends Pilot Knob, setting sight on the fertile hunting grounds of what is now known as the Bluegrass Region of the State of Kentucky [exact date disputed]
Thomas Malthuspublishes the first edition of his influential 'Essay on the Principle of Population' (date of the unsigned preface)
Anglo-Canadian explorerDavid Thompsonreaches the mouth of the Saskatchewan River in Manitoba
Union GeneralBenjamin Butlerorders William Mumford hanged after he removed and destroyed US flag on display over New Orleans Mint
Battle of Milliken's Bend, Lousisiana,Jefferson Davis' home burnt
Abraham Lincolnrenominated for US President by the Republican Party
Gandhiis thrown off a First Class compartment of a Pretoria train in Pietermaritzburg, South Africa - leads to his first act of civil disobedience when he protests his treatment the next day
Mary Pickfordmakes her screen debut at the age of 16
Theodore Rooseveltdeclines nomination of the Progressive Party and throws his support behind Republican Charles Evans Hughes
George Leigh-Mallorydisappears 775' from Everest's summit
George Balanchineand Kurt Weills' ballet chanté "7 Deadly Sins" premieres in Paris
Charles "Lucky" Lucianois convicted on 62 counts of compulsory prostitution
TheDouglas DC-4E makes its 1st test flight
George VIand Elizabeth become the 1st king and queen of Britain to visit USA

ChemistsArcher John Porter Martinand Richard L. M. Synge give the first demonstration of partition chromatography (separation of mixtures) at a meeting of the Biochemical Society held at the National Institute for Medical Research, Hampstead
Battle of Midway ends: AdmiralChester Nimitzwins 1st World War II naval defeat of Japan
First President to appear on color TV (Eisenhower)
India Prime MinisterJawaharlal Nehruvisits the Soviet Union
Gene Roddenberryresigns from the Los Angeles Police Department to concentrate on his writing career
American songwriter and impresarioBerry GordyJr. founds Tamla Records, precursor to Motown Records, in Detroit, Michigan
NASA civilian test pilotJoseph A. Walkertakes X-15 to 31,580 m
MLB Draft: New York Mets pass onReggie Jacksonto select Antelope Valley HS catcher Steve Chilcott #1
Sirhan Sirhanindicted for the assassination of US SenatorRobert F. Kennedy
"Johnny CashShow" debuts on ABC-TV
Bob DylanandJohnny Cashperform duet on a "Grand Ole Opry" TV special
Supergroup Blind Faith's debut performance in Hyde Park, London, featuringEric Clapton, Steve Winwood, Steve Baler and Rick Grech
American jockeyBill Shoemaker(38) passesJohnny Longdenwith his 6,033 career win
West German ChancellorWilly Brandtvisits Israel
"Thank God I'm a Country Boy" byJohn Denverhits #1
Dennis Lilleetakes the first one-day 5-wicket haul, 5-34 against Pakistan in the Cricket World Cup
"NBC Nightly News" changes to a dual-anchor show with John Chancellor andDavid Brinkley
During the Queen's Jubilee, theSex Pistolsattempt to perform on a boat on the River Thames, but are forced to stop by the police
Rock 'n' Roll singer-songwriter and guitar legendChuck Berryis charged with tax evasion
Tommy Johnwins his 200th, 3-0 on a 2-hitter
Graceland,Elvis Presley's home, is opened to the public
Hissène Habré's rebel army occupies N'djamena the capital of Chad
LA DodgerSteve Garveyis 5th to play in 1,000 consecutive games
NY Mets draftDwight Gooden, Roger McDowell & Randy Myers
US PresidentRonald Reaganmeets with PopeJohn Paul IIat Vatican City, Rome and laterQueen Elizabeth II
MLB Philadelphia PhilliesSteve Carltontemporarily passes Houston AstroNolan Ryanwith his 3,552 strike out
French Open Women's Tennis:Chris EvertbeatsMartina Navratilova2-6, 6-3, 6-3 for her 18th and final Grand Slam title and record 7th French singles crown
Madonna's single "Live to Tell" goes #1
Wayne Gretzkywins his 9th NHL Hart (MVP) Trophy in 10 years
Michael Jacksonenters St. John's Hospital and Health Center, near his home, for chest pains after a dance rehearsal and exercise session
South African PresidentF. W. de Klerklifts 4 year state of emergency
MLB Oakland A's outfielderRickey Hendersonsteals his 1,100th career base
Tony Blair's Labour Party wins another landslide victory in the General Election
"Live Like You Were Dying" single released byTim McGraw(Grammy Award Best Country Song, 2004; Billboard Song of the Year, 2004)
MLB Draft: Southern Nevada outfielderBryce Harperfirst pick by Washington Nationals
16th century archaeology remains of the Curtain Theatre, where some ofShakespeare's plays first performed found under a pub in London
Iris Apfelis awarded the Women Together Special Award of the Year at the 12th Annual Women Together Gala in New York City
Suicide bombers attack Iranian parliament in Tehran and the mausoleum ofAyatollah Khomeinikilling 12, 1st Islamic State attacks in Iran
US Vice PresidentKamala Harrisvisiting Guatemala on her first foreign trip, announces task forces on corruption and human trafficking
At 98, actor and comedianDick Van Dykebecomes the oldest winner of a Daytime Emmy, for a guest performance in "Days of Our Lives"
Pat Sajak’s final appearance on "Wheel of Fortune" after 43 years - the longest continuous stint by a game show host
George Clooney's stage drama "Good Night, and Good Luck" about journalistEdward R. Murrow's 1950s stand against anti-communist witch hunts is first live broadcast of a Broadway performance, on CNN
US PresidentDonald Trumporders deployment of 2,000 National Guard troops to southern California to quell protests against Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) sweeps in and around Los Angeles
Treaty of Tordesillas: Spain and Portugal divide the New World along a meridian 370 leagues west of the Cape Verde Islands off the west coast of Africa

Great Plague of London:Samuel Pepyswrites in his diary of houses marked with a red cross in London's Drury Lane, meaning somebody inside is infected with the plague and must be locked in for 40 days or until death
British Museum is founded by an Act of Parliament with royal assent from KingGeorge II, opens in 1759
Richard Lee (Virginia) moves Declaration of Independence in Continental Congress
Time magazine publishes the second of the only two known photos taken of the United States Supreme Court in session
New York's air quality sinks to the lowest in the world (air quality index 218) as smoke from Canadian wildfires blankets the American north east
Huizong, Chinese Emperor, 8th Emperor of the Song Dynasty (1100-26), born in Bianliang, Henan, China (d. 1135)
John III, King of Portugal and the Algarves (1521-57), born in the Palace of Alcáçova, Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1557)
Étienne Pasquier, French lawyer and man of letters, born in Paris (d. 1615)
Paulus Voet, Dutch jurist and historian, born in Heusden, Netherlands (d. 1667)
Georg von Pasterwiz, Austrian composer, born in Bierhütten, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1803)
Karl Frieberth, Austrian composer, born in Wullersdorf, Austria (d. 1816)
John Rennie, Scottish engineer, born in East Linton, East Lothian, Scotland (d. 1821)
James Young Simpson, Scottish obstetrician who popularized chloroform for medicinal use, born in Bathgate, West Lothian, Scotland (d. 1870)
Theophilus Toulmin Garrard, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Clay County, Kentucky (d. 1902)
R. D. Blackmore, English novelist (Norie, Lorna Doone), born in Longworth, Berkshire, England (d. 1900)
Amelia Edwards, English author (The Phantom Coach) and Egyptologist, born in London (d. 1892)
Alexander Ritter, German composer, born in Narva, Estonia (d. 1896)
Alois Hitler, father of Adolf Hitler, born in Strones, Waldviertel, Austrian Empire (d. 1903)
Carlota of Mexico, princess of Belgium and empress consort of Mexico (1864-67), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1927)
Leopold von Auer, Hungarian-American violinist, born in Veszprém, Hungary (d. 1930)
Władysław Górski, Polish violinist, composer and teacher, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1915)
Philipp Lenard, Austrian physicist (Nobel 1905), born in Pozsony, Austria-Hungary (d. 1947)
Luigi Maurizio Tedeschi, Italian composer, born in Turin, Italy (d. 1944)
Charles Rennie Mackintosh, Scottish architect, designer, and illustrator, born in Townhead, Glasgow (d. 1928)
Landon Ronald, British composer, pianist and teacher (Guildhall School of Music), born in Kensington, London (d. 1938)
Theodor Streicher, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1940)
George Simpson-Hayward, English cricket spin bowler (5 Tests, 23 wickets @ 18.26; last great underarm bowler; Cambridge University CC, Worcestershire CCC), born in Stoneleigh, England (d. 1936)
Charles Glover Barkla, English physicist (1917 Nobel Prize for Physics for X-ray scattering), born in Widnes, England (d. 1944)
Joan Voûte, Dutch astronomer, born in Madioen, Java (d. 1963)
Knud J. V. Rasmussen, Danish pole explorer (Thule), born in Jakobshavn, North Greenland, Kingdom of Denmark (d. 1933)
Kanno Sugako, Japanese anarcho-feminist journalist, hanged for her role in the plot to assassinate Emperor Meiji, born in 881, Kita Ward, Osaka, Japan (d. 1911)
Sylvanus Morley, American archaeologist and spy, born in Chester, Pennsylvania (d. 1948)
Julius P. Hoste, Belgian journalist, daily newspaper publisher (Last News), and politician (Minister of Education, 1936-38; Senator 1949-54), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1954)
J.B. Morton, British satirist under pen name of Beachcomber (Daily Express "By the Way" column, 1924-75), born in Tooting, England (d. 1979)
Percy Brier, Australian composer, born in North Pine, Queensland, Australia (d. 1970)
Henri Coandă, Romanian aerodynamics pioneer, born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 1972)
Willem Walraven, Dutch journalist and writer (De Indische Courant / The Indies Newspaper; Brieven / Letters), born in Goeree-Overvlakkee, Netherlands (d. 1943)[1]
Athos Palma, Argentine composer, born in Buenos Aires< Argentina (d. 1951)
Gillis Grafström, Swedish figure skater (Olympic gold 1920, 24, 28), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1938)
Douglas Campbell, American aviator and World War I flying ace, born in San Francisco, California (d. 1990)
Robert S. Mulliken, American chemist and physicist (Nobel 1966), born in Newburyport, Massachusetts (d. 1986)
Vivien Kellems, American industrialist, who battled the Federal tax system as discriminatory against single people, born in Des Moines, Iowa (d. 1975)
George Szell, Hungarian-born American conductor (Cleveland Orchestra, 1946-70), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1970)
Elizabeth Bowen, Anglo-Irish novelist (The Death of the Heart, Encounters), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1973)
John Hawkes, Australian tennis player (Australasian C'ship singles, doubles, mixed doubles 1926), born in Geelong, Victoria (d. 1990)
Glen Gray [Knoblauch], American jazz saxophonist and bandleader (Casa Loma Orchestra), born in Metamora, Illinois (d. 1963)
Jan Engelman, Dutch poet, translator and critic (Garden of Eros), born in Utrecht, the Netherlands (d. 1972)
(Sarah) Hope Summers, American character actress (The Andy Griffith Show & Mayberry RFD - "Clara"), born in Mattoon, Illinois (d. 1979)
Herman B. Wells, president and chancellor of Indiana University, born in Jamestown, Indiana (d. 2000)
Boris Goldovsky, Russian-American conductor, educator (Tangle wood, 1942-62), and opera broadcast commentator (Metropolitan Opera, 1946-85), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 2001)
Peter W. Rodino, American politician, U.S. House of Representatives from New Jersey (1949-89), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 2005)
Virginia Apgar, American physician and childbirth specialist, born in Westfield, New Jersey (d. 1974)
Bradford Washburn, American explorer and cartographer, born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 2007)
Marion Post Wolcott, American photographer during the Great Depression, born in Montclair, New Jersey (d. 1990)
Brooks Stevens, American automotive designer (1949 Hydra-Glide Harley; 1963 Jeep Wagoneer), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 1995)
Franz Reizenstein, German-British pianist, composer (Concerto Popolare), and educator, born in Nuremberg, Germany (d. 1968)
Silas Roy Crain, American gospel singer, founder of The Soul Stirrers Gospel Quartet, born in Augustine, Texas (d. 1996)
Dallas Shirley, American Basketball Hall of Fame referee (officiated 2,000+ games in 33-year career), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1994)
Gwendolyn Brooks, American poet (The Bean Eaters, Annie Allen, Pulitzer 1950), born in Topeka, Kansas (d. 2000)
Georges Marchais, French political leader (French Communist Party), born in La Hoguette, France (d. 1997)
Richard M. Givan, American jurist (Indiana Supreme Court, 1969-94), born in Indianapolis, Indians (d. 2009)
Tal Farlow, American jazz guitarist, born in Greensboro, North Carolina (d. 1998)
Beryl Booker, American swing and cool jazz pianist (Dinah Washington), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1978)
Hubert Du Plessis, South African pianist and composer, born in Malmesbury, Cape Province, Union of South Africa (d. 2011)
Giorgio Belladonna, Italian bridge player (16 x World C'ships; 10 x European C'ships, Italy open team-of-four), born in Rome (d. 1995)
Jules Deschênes, Canadian Quebec Superior Court judge, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2000)
Dolores Gray [Sylvia Dolores Finkelstein], American Tony Award-winning stage and screen singer and actress (Designing Woman; Kismet; Destry Rides Again), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2002)
Dick Williams, American singer and choral director (The Andy Williams Show), born in Wall Lake, Iowa (d. 2018)
Martin Carter, Guyanese poet and political activist, born in Georgetown, Guyana (d. 1997)
Anthony Nicholas Maria Wahl, American historian, born in New York (d. 1996)
Anthony Ortega, American session and jazz clarinetist, saxophonist, and flautist (A Man and His Horns), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2022)
Dave Bowen, Welsh soccer wing-half (19 caps; Arsenal) and manager (Wales 1964-74; Northampton Town), born in Maesteg, Wales (d. 1995)
David Malcolm Lewis, English historian, expert in Greek Epigraphy, born in London (d. 1994)
James Ivory, American film director and screenwriter (Merchant Ivory Productions - Howard's End; Remains of the Day; A Room with a View), born in Berkeley, California
James Jude, American thoracic surgeon (developed CPR), born in Maple Lake, Minnesota (d. 2015)
Randy Turpin, British boxer (NYSAC, NBA and The Ring middleweight titles 1951-53), born in Leamington Spa, England (d. 1966)
Reg Park, British bodybuilder (Mr Universe - 1951, 58, 65), born in Leeds, West Yorkshire, England (d. 2007)
Antonio Carbajal, Mexican soccer goalkeeper (32 caps; Club León 364 games) and manager (León, Unión de Curtidores, Atlético Morelia), born in Mexico City (d. 2023)
Hilderaldo Bellini, Brazilian soccer defender (51 caps; Vasco da Gama, São Paulo), born in Itapira, Brazil (d. 2014)
Lang Jeffries, Canadian actor (Rescue 8 - "Skip"), born in Ontario (d. 1987)
(Nii Moi) "Speedy" Acquaye, Ghanaian drummer, percussionist and fire-eater (Tubby Hayes; Georgie Fame and the Blue Flames; Ginger Baker's Airforce), born in Accra, Gold Coast (d. 1993)
David Driskell, American artist and champion of African American art, born in Eatonton, Georgia (d. 2020)
Malcolm Morley, English-American painter, born in London (d. 2018)
Michel Le Milinaire, French soccer coach (Laval 1968-92, Rennes 1993-96), born in Kergrist-Moëlou, France (d. 2023)
Virginia McKenna, British actress (Born Free, Chosen, Lions are Free, Simba), born in Marylebone, London
(Harold) "Tina" Brooks, American hard-bop jazz saxophonist, and composer ("True Blue"), born in Fayetteville, North Carolina (d. 1974)
Joel Collier, American football coach (HC Buffalo Bills 1966-68; DC Denver Broncos 1972–88), born in Rock Island, Illinois (d. 2024)
Henk Koning, Dutch State Secretary of Finances (VVD), born in Beilen, Netherlands (d. 2016)
Herb Score, American baseball pitcher (MLB All-Star 1955, 56; Cleveland Indians), and broadcaster (Cleveland Indians, 1964-97), born in Rosedale, New York (d. 2008)
Beverly Wills, American actress (I Married Joan - "Beverly"; Some Like It Hot), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1963)
Philippe Entremont, French pianist and conductor (Vienna Chamber Orchestra, 1976-91), born in Reims, France
Samuel Lipman, American pianist and music critic, born in Los Gatos, California (d. 1994)
Harry Crews, American writer and actor (Indian Runner), born in Alma, Georgia (d. 2012)
Thomas Kailath, American electrical engineer, born in Pune, Maharashtra, India
Kenneth Nichols, Canadian classical pianist, composer (Symphonic Celebration; Boy In A Cage), and teacher (Brandon University, 1962-96), born in Pincher Creek, Alberta (d. 2021)
Bert Sugar, American sports writer (editor / publisher The Ring magazine; Sting like a Bee; The Ageless Warrior), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 2012)
Neeme Järvi, Estonian-American conductor (Gothenburg Symphony, 1982-2004; Detroit Symphony, 1990-2005; Residentie Orkest, 2005-12), born in Tallinn, Estonia
Red Grooms [Charles Grooms], American multimedia artist (Ruckus Manhattan), born in Nashville, Tennessee
Ian St John, Scottish soccer forward (21 caps; Motherwell; Liverpool 425 games), manager (Motherwell, Portsmouth) and broadcaster (ITV: Saint and Greavsie), born in Motherwell, Scotland (d. 2021)
Idris Sardi, Indonesian violinist and composer, noted for his film scores, born in Jakarta, Indonesia (d. 2014)
